paper_name: The Effect of Restrictive vs Liberal Blood Transfusion Strategy on Subsequent Myocardial Infarction Type
clinical_summary_markdown:
### Background
Data on the differential impact of interventions on subsequent myocardial infarction (MI) type are limited.

### Objectives
This post-hoc analysis evaluated the 30-day rate of subsequent MI by type (type 1 and 2) among patients enrolled in the MINT (Myocardial Ischemia and Transfusion; NCT02981407) trial.

### Methods
Subdistribution HRs and cumulative incidences of subsequent MI types were computed using Fine-Gray subdistribution models, accounting for competing risks of death and other MI types. Effect modification of treatment strategy by index MI type was tested using log-binomial regression models.

### Results
Among 3,504 MINT trial patients, 275 (7.8%) experienced a 30-day subsequent MI. Of these, 118 (43%) were type 2 MI, 79 (28%) were uncertain MI type, 40 (15%) were type 4 MI, and 38 (14%) were type 1 MI. The rate of subsequent type 2 MI in patients randomized to the restrictive vs liberal transfusion strategy was 3.5% (n = 61) vs 3.2% (n = 57) (HR: 1.07; 95% CI: 0.74-1.53). The rate of subsequent type 1 MI was 1.3% (n = 23) vs 0.9% (n = 15) (HR: 1.53; 95% CI: 0.80-2.94).

### Conclusions
Among patients with MI and anemia, subsequent MI occurred within 30 days in 7.8% of patients, with type 2 MI occurring 3 times more often than type 1 MI. No differential effect of the restrictive vs liberal transfusion strategy on the type of subsequent MI (e.g., type 1 vs type 2) was observed.
